column1: Kashmir Shaivism and Trika Lineage: Gurus, Texts, and Teachings

column3: Supreme deity, source of all transmissions
column5: Ultimate non-dual reality, source of all manifestation
column6: Revealed original tantras through his five faces

column7: Referenced in Netra Tantra, Svacchanda Tantra (Flood, 1993)

column6: Hot-tempered sage, embodiment of Śiva's anger
Three-eyed sage, received knowledge from gods
Discovered Śiva Sūtras engraved on stone at Mahadeva mountain
column7: Historical references in commentaries, Kashmiri chronicles (Sanderson, 1985)

column6: Seen as incarnation of Kartikeya, Śiva's son

column5: Recognition philosophy, critique of Buddhist logic

column3: Disciple of Utpaladeva, teacher of Abhinavagupta

column6: Used poison-eating practice to attain siddhi
From South India, initiated Abhinavagupta into Trika-Kula
column7: Historical figure, mentioned extensively in Tantrāloka (Sanderson, 1988)

column4: Tantrāloka, Tantrasāra, Parātriṃśikāvivaraṇa, Īśvarapratyabhijñāvimarśinī, Abhinavabhāratī, Parātrīśikālaghuvṛtti, Mālinīvijayavārttika, Paramārthasāra, Bodhapañcadaśikā, Ghaṭakarpara-kulakakāvyaṭīkā, Kramastotra
column5: Complete integration of Trika, Krama, Pratyabhijñā, and Kula; aesthetics theory; sophisticated ritual theory
column6: Said to have entered Bhairava cave with 1,200 disciples and disappeared; considered an incarnation of Śiva
column7: Multiple historical sources, most comprehensive Kashmir Shaiva philosopher (Rastogi, 1987; Gnoli, 1999; Sanderson, 1987)

column4: Śivasūtravimarśinī, Spandanirṇaya, Pratyabhijñāhṛdaya, Svacchandatantroddyota, Netratantroddyota, Vijñānabhairavadīpikā, Stavacintāmaṇi, Parāprāveśikā
column5: Accessible explanations of non-dual Shaivism, commentaries on core texts

column4: Kashmir Shaivism: The Secret Supreme, commentaries on classical texts
column6: Considered last living master in direct lineage

column7: Historical figure, founded Siddha Yoga (Brooks et al., 1997)

column4: Kundalini: The Evolutionary Energy in Man
Translations of Śiva Sūtras, Spanda Kārikās, Vijñāna Bhairava, Pratyabhijñāhṛdayam

column1: Anuttara: The Absolute, beyond which nothing exists
Paramaśiva: Supreme consciousness as unified reality
Prakāśa: Light of consciousness, self-illuminating awareness
Cicchakti: Power of consciousness to manifest reality
Spanda: Divine vibration or pulsation of consciousness
Ābhāsavāda: Doctrine of manifestation as appearance
36 Tattvas: Principles of manifestation from Śiva to Earth
Māyā: Creative power that generates differentiation
Trika: Three-fold division: Śiva, Śakti, Nara (individual)
Five Acts (Pañcakṛtya): Creation, maintenance, dissolution, concealment, grace
Pratyabhijñā: Recognition of one's identity with Śiva
Kaula: Embodied practice integrating all aspects of experience
Kula: Total integration of reality in consciousness
Spanda Texts: Focusing on divine vibration
Pratyabhijñā Texts: Focusing on recognition philosophy
Krama Texts: Focusing on sequential realization
Kula Texts: Focusing on embodied totality
Mālinīvijayottara Tantra: Primary source text for Trika
Svacchanda Tantra: Important for ritual elements
Netra Tantra: Focuses on protective practices and subtle anatomy
Rudrayāmala Tantra: Early source for Trika-Kaula synthesis
Parātriṃśikā: Concise revelation on supreme reality
Śiva Sūtras: Revealed aphorisms attributed to Śiva via Vasugupta
Spandakārikā: Verses on divine vibration by Vasugupta/Kallata
Śivadṛṣṭi: Somānanda's foundational text on divine perception
Pratyabhijñāhṛdaya: Kṣemarāja's concise summary in 20 sutras
